question: [File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary's liveness check (age > 2*time.Minute) uses a fixed 2-minute window with no configurable threshold, so that in high-latency environments where the heartbeat goroutine is delayed by network I/O, the probe consistently fails and causes a restart loop that keeps network nodes busy with repeated connection teardown and setup?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary writes 'heartbeat stale' to stderr when the age exceeds 2*time.Minute, and if stderr is captured by a log aggregator that triggers automated remediation (e.g., pod restart), cause a feedback loop where each restart generates more 'heartbeat stale' messages, amplifying pod restarts and network node connection load?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary's 'live' mode calls os.Stat('/tmp/alive') which is a blocking syscall, and if /tmp is on a FUSE filesystem that can be made to hang, cause the liveness probe to time out (rather than return exit 1), which Kubernetes treats as a failure and triggers a pod restart that floods network nodes?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary has no circuit-breaker logic, so that if network nodes are already under load and responding slowly, the pinger's retry logic (submitWithRetry) keeps sending more transactions, and the healthcheck binary continues to report the pod as live and ready, preventing any automatic scale-down that would reduce node load?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary's 'ready' mode exits 0 immediately upon finding /tmp/ready, without checking whether the pinger has successfully completed even one transaction, so that a pod that is misconfigured to target a high-load network node is immediately marked ready and starts sending transactions, amplifying that node's load?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary's mode selection (os.Args[1]) is case-sensitive with no normalization, so that passing 'Live' or 'LIVE' falls through to the default branch and exits with code 2, which some orchestrators interpret as a fatal probe error causing immediate pod termination and restart, amplifying network node connection load?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary performs no PID file check or process existence check, so that if the main pinger process has crashed but /tmp/alive was recently written, the liveness probe returns 0 (alive) while no transactions are being submitted, masking a state where the pinger will restart and send a burst of transactions to network nodes upon recovery?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary's 'live' mode computes age = time.Since(fi.ModTime()) which uses wall clock time, and if the container's wall clock is set forward (e.g., via a misconfigured time sync), the age appears larger than actual, causing false liveness failures and pod restarts that increase network node load?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary's 'live' mode does not distinguish between a missing /tmp/alive file and a permission-denied error on os.Stat, so that if /tmp/alive permissions are changed to 000, the probe fails with a permission error and triggers a pod restart, causing network nodes to handle new connection bursts?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary's 'ready' mode does not check /tmp/alive freshness, so that a pod where the heartbeat has stopped (pinger goroutine hung) is still marked ready, causing the orchestrator to keep routing work to it, and when the pinger eventually recovers it fires all queued ticks simultaneously at network nodes?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary's liveness probe failure causes Kubernetes to restart the container (not just the pod), and each container restart causes the pinger's main() to call newClient() which calls buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() with up to mirrorNodeClientMaxRetries+1 HTTP requests to the mirror node, amplifying mirror node and network node load?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary's 'live' mode prints the raw os.Stat error (fmt.Fprintln(os.Stderr, err)) which may include filesystem paths or inode information, and use this information to craft a more targeted attack on the /tmp filesystem to cause persistent liveness failures and a sustained pod restart loop?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary has no exponential backoff or cooldown between successive probe invocations (that is controlled externally by Kubernetes probe settings), so that if the probe interval is set very low, the healthcheck binary is invoked many times per second, each time calling os.Stat('/tmp/alive'), creating filesystem I/O pressure that delays the heartbeat write and causes liveness failures?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary's 'live' mode does not validate that /tmp/alive contains a valid RFC3339Nano timestamp (only ModTime is checked), so that replacing /tmp/alive with a file containing malformed content but a fresh timestamp still passes the liveness check, masking a state where the pinger is misconfigured and sending malformed transactions to network nodes?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary's 'ready' mode and 'live' mode check different files (/tmp/ready vs /tmp/alive) with no cross-validation, so that a pod can be simultaneously marked ready (for traffic) but not live (triggering restart), causing a window where the pod receives traffic while being restarted, and the in-flight transactions are retried against network nodes?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary's default mode (when no args are given) is 'ready', so that an orchestrator that invokes the healthcheck without arguments always gets a readiness check, and if /tmp/ready exists but the pinger is in a retry storm, the pod is kept ready while hammering network nodes with failed transaction retries?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary's 'live' mode uses os.Stat which does not lock the file, so that a race condition between the healthcheck reading ModTime and the heartbeat goroutine writing /tmp/alive causes the healthcheck to see a partially written file with an incorrect ModTime, leading to a false liveness failure and pod restart?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary's 'live' mode does not check whether the pinger's context has been cancelled (ctx.Done()), so that a pod that is shutting down (context cancelled) still passes the liveness check as long as /tmp/alive is fresh, causing the orchestrator to delay termination and keep the pod sending transactions to network nodes during shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary's 'ready' mode exits 0 as long as /tmp/ready exists, even if the pinger's interval has been set to a very small value (e.g., 1ms via HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_INTERVAL), causing the pinger to submit transactions at an extremely high rate to network nodes, increasing their processing load by over 30%?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary's 'live' mode's 2-minute staleness window is much larger than the 15-second heartbeat interval, creating a 7.5x safety margin that masks scenarios where the heartbeat goroutine is running but the transaction ticker is blocked, allowing the pinger to appear live while accumulating deferred transactions that will burst onto network nodes?
